text: Every equine animal must be identified by a single lifetime identification document (equine passport) issued before the animal is six months old or by 31 December in its year of birth, whichever is later.
A keeper must apply for a duplicate identification document where the original is lost, and the animal must be signed out of the human food chain when issued a duplicate.
The keeper must notify the relevant passport issuing organisation of the death of an equine animal within 30 days.
An equine animal moved into Great Britain must be registered on the Central Equine Database within 30 days of import.
A microchip (transponder) implanted by a veterinary surgeon must be recorded in the identification document.
Imported animals must be accompanied by an animal health certificate signed by an official veterinarian of the exporting country.
Importers must give the competent authority at least 24 hours advance notice of the arrival of a consignment of animals.
Animals must enter Great Britain through a designated border control post appropriate to the species.
Operators must ensure that kept terrestrial animals are accompanied by an animal health certificate when moved between territories.
Equidae must come from an establishment that is not subject to movement restrictions for equine diseases.
Equidae must be examined by an official veterinarian within 48 hours before loading for export.
The appropriate authority may grant derogations from import conditions for the temporary admission of registered horses.
No person shall transport animals in a way likely to cause injury or unnecessary suffering to them.
Animals must be fit for the intended journey and transported under conditions guaranteeing their welfare.
Long journeys of more than eight hours require an approved transporter authorisation and a journey log.
Registered horses for temporary admission must return within 90 days of entry into the territory.
Equidae for import must undergo a test for equine infectious anaemia (Coggins test) with a negative result.
Equidae must have resided for at least 40 days in the exporting country before export, or since birth if younger.
A residence in an equine-disease-free zone must be certified for animals coming from listed third countries.
The Minister may make orders to prevent the introduction or spreading of disease through imported animals.
An inspector may seize and detain an animal reasonably suspected of being affected with a notifiable disease.
Purebred breeding equidae must be accompanied by a zootechnical certificate when entering Great Britain.
Breed societies must maintain a breeding book recognised by the competent authority.
A slurry storage system must have a minimum storage capacity of four months for pig or poultry slurry and five months for other livestock.
A slurry storage tank must be designed and constructed so that no part of it is within 10 metres of inland or coastal waters.
The base and walls of a slurry storage system must be impermeable and protected against corrosion for at least 20 years with maintenance.
Earth-banked slurry lagoons must have a minimum freeboard of 750 millimetres at all times.
Organic manures must not be applied to agricultural land where the soil is waterlogged, flooded, snow-covered, or frozen for more than 12 hours in the previous 24.
Organic manures must not be stored on land within 10 metres of inland or coastal waters or within 50 metres of a spring, well, or borehole.
Applications of organic manure must be planned to meet soil and crop nutrient need, taking account of soil nutrient supply and other applications.
The total nitrogen from livestock manure applied on the holding in any 12-month period must not exceed 170 kilograms per hectare of the holding area.
High readily-available nitrogen organic manures must not be spread on grassland in a Nitrate Vulnerable Zone during the closed period from 15 September to 31 December.
High readily-available nitrogen organic manures must not be spread on tillage land in a Nitrate Vulnerable Zone during the closed period from 1 August to 31 December.
A farmer must keep a record of livestock numbers, manure produced, manure imported and exported, and nitrogen applications for at least five years.
An intensive pig or poultry installation above specified thresholds must hold an environmental permit before operating.
The operator of a permitted installation must apply Best Available Techniques to prevent or minimise emissions of ammonia from housing, storage and spreading.
A permit holder must monitor and report ammonia emissions in accordance with the conditions set by the regulator.
Digestate and compost derived from category 3 animal by-products must meet the microbiological standards set out in the retained EU Animal By-Products Regulation before being placed on the market.
Animal by-products and derived products must be collected, transported, stored and disposed of so as to avoid risks to public and animal health.
A local authority may serve an abatement notice on the operator of an agricultural premises where ammonia or odour emissions constitute a statutory nuisance.
Any smell, fumes or gases emitted from livestock or slurry premises so as to be prejudicial to health or a nuisance is a statutory nuisance that the local authority must take steps to abate.
Sewage sludge must not be applied to agricultural land unless the soil has been tested and the concentration of regulated heavy metals does not exceed the prescribed limits.
A producer or user of sewage sludge must keep records of the quantity, composition and the land on which it has been applied for at least 10 years.
